elements.sent-2411-001: Underground hydrogen storage in saline aquifers is a potential solution for seasonal renewable energy storage.
elements.sent-2411-002: Among potential storage sites, facilities used for underground natural gas storage have advantages, including well-characterized cyclical injection-withdrawal behavior and partially reusable infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, the differences between hydrogen-brine and natural gas-brine flow, particularly through fractures in the reservoir and the sealing caprock, remain unclear due to the complexity of two-phase flow.
elements.sent-2411-004: Therefore, we investigate fracture relative permeability for hydrogen versus methane (natural gas) and nitrogen (commonly used in laboratories).
elements.sent-2411-005: Steady-state relative permeability experiments were conducted at 10 MPa on fractured carbonate rock from the Loenhout natural gas storage in Belgium, where gas flows through {\textmu m-to-mm scale fractures.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results reveal that the hydrogen exhibits similar relative permeability curves to methane, but both are significantly lower than those measured for nitrogen.
elements.sent-2411-007: This implies that nitrogen cannot reliably serve as a proxy for hydrogen at typical reservoir pressures.
elements.sent-2411-008: The low relative permeabilities for hydrogen and methane indicate strong fluid phase interference, which traditional relative permeability models fail to capture.
elements.sent-2411-009: This is supported by our observation of periodic pressure fluctuations associated with intermittent fluid connectivity for hydrogen and methane.
elements.sent-2411-010: In conclusion, our findings suggest that the fundamental flow properties of fractured rocks are complex but relatively similar for hydrogen and natural gas.
elements.sent-2411-011: This is an important insight for predictive modeling of the conversion of Loenhout and similar natural gas storage facilities, which is crucial to evaluate their hydrogen storage efficiency and integrity.
elements.sent-2411-012: The hydrogen economy is expected to play a critical role in the global transition to cleaner energy and achieving net-zero carbon emissions.
elements.sent-2411-013: A key technology supporting large-scale energy storage is Underground Hydrogen Storage (UHS).
elements.sent-2411-014: UHS enables the storage of TWh-scale energy by injecting hydrogen into depleted gas reservoirs, saline aquifers, and existing gas storage facilities.
elements.sent-2411-015: The stored (green) hydrogen can be produced by electrolysis during periods of surplus electricity generation \cite{Tarkowski2019, Tarhan2021, Zivar2021, Thiyagarajan2022, Al-Shafi2023, Perera2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: As renewable energy sources like wind and solar continue to expand \cite{Acar2014 , UHS provides a potential solution to balance renewable energy supply and demand, enhancing energy security, and helping to decarbonize energy systems.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Converting pre-existing natural gas storage sites to hydrogen storage potentially offers advantages to using depleted gas fields or saline aquifers that have not been previously deployed for storage purposes.
elements.sent-2411-018: This includes leveraging expensive site characterization campaigns, tried-and-tested reservoir and caprock behaviour under cyclical injection-retraction operations, and existing connections to gas treatment and pipeline infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-019: Among operational and planned natural gas storage sites in Europe, 86\% are depleted gas fields and 14\% are aquifers, together capable of providing up to 750 TWh of hydrogen storage \cite{HyUSPre2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Conversion of these storage resources would be sufficient for a mid-range scenario of 2,500 TWh of annual hydrogen demand by 2050, at a 30\% storage capacity.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we focus on a notable example of such a natural gas storage site under investigation for hydrogen conversion: the Loenhout natural gas storage facility in northern Belgium.
elements.sent-2411-022: Loenhout is uniquely well-connected in Western Europe as it is part of a pipeline network linking Belgium, France, The Netherlands, Germany, Luxemburg and the United Kingdom, including international shipping terminals in the North Sea ports of Zeebrugge and Dunkirk.
elements.sent-2411-023: If converted succesfully, it would provide 2.2 TWh of storage capacity.
elements.sent-2411-024: The site comprises a partially karstified limestone aquifer from Visean age, characterized by a fracture-dominated flow system with low primary porosity \cite{Dreesen1987, Lagrou2012, Vandenberghe2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Its conversion is currently under investigation in the BE-Hystore project funded by the Belgian federal Energy Transition Fund and the site operator Fluxys Belgium.
elements.sent-2411-026: Repurposing an aquifer natural gas storage site for large-scale hydrogen injection requires evaluating whether the flow of the stored gas in the pores or fractures of the storage reservoir would be affected, as this may impact injectivity and operational efficiency \citep{Heinemann2021a, Lysyy2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: While significant progress has been made in understanding hydrogen flow in porous media, substantial knowledge gaps remain regarding its behavior in fractured rock.
elements.sent-2411-028: This is not only important for fractured storage reservoirs such as in Loenhout, but also to assess leakage through caprock if fractures are present or generated during storage \cite{Aftab2022, Aslannezhad2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: A primary uncertainty in the current scientific understanding lies in hydrogen's relative permeability compared to methane’s.
elements.sent-2411-030: Relative permeability is a key parameter for upscaled multiphase flow modeling, which quantifies how the flow of gas is impacted by the presence of (saline) groundwater in the reservoir, and vice versa \citep{Blunt2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Over the last two decades, several studies have shown that the relative permeability behaviour of fractured media differs significantly from that of porous materials due to its dependence on complex fluid dynamics \citep{Phillips2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Nevertheless, it is currently unclear how such complexities affect fracture relative permeabilities for hydrogen compared to other gases, particularly at elevated pressures and temperatures typical for UHS.
elements.sent-2411-033: Experiments with air-water in transparent fractures have revealed strong phase interference and reduced relative permeability at intermediate saturations in rough fractures \citep{Pruess1990, Persoff1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Relative permeability in such systems is highly sensitive to fracture surface roughness and aperture heterogeneity, influencing the meandering or twisting of flow paths \citep{Chen2006, Huo2016, Nicholl2000, Yang2013, Ye2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Capillary-dominated flow, especially in low viscosity-ratio fluid systems such as hydrogen-brine and methane-brine, is characterized by fluid pathways that are intermittently connected, caused by snap-off events and Haines jumps \citep{Phillips2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: State-of-the-art fracture relative permeability models aim to capture the greater phase interference, the absence of percolating pathways, and altered flow patterns compared to porous media \citep{Hatami2022, Watanabe2015, Wang2018, Gong2021, Eliebid2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The current need for understanding how the findings above translate to UHS is underscored by a lack of experimental studies on fractured rock.
elements.sent-2411-038: This is despite significant recent efforts on the corresponding behaviour of porous reservoir rocks \citep{Yekta2018, Lysyy2022, Boon2022, Jangda2023, Higgs2024, Higgs2023, Bo2023, Rezaei2022 , which are difficult to translate directly to fracture systems.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Comparative studies of two-phase flow behavior of relevant fractured samples under consistent reservoir conditions are thus critical to address these uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-040: Assessing hydrogen flow in fractured aquifers such as Loenhout is essential to evaluate its feasibility as a storage medium in comparison to methane.
elements.sent-2411-041: Therefore, our study presents the first direct measurements of drainage relative permeability for hydrogen-brine, methane-brine, and nitrogen-brine systems in fractured limestone rock at subsurface pressure conditions of 10 MPa.
elements.sent-2411-042: The investigated rock used was collected from a depth of \SI{1400 {\meter at the Loenhout aquifer.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Methane was selected because it is a good model for the stored gas in current natural gas storage aquifers \citep{Hashemi2021 , and it may serve effectively as a cushion gas.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Nitrogen was selected because of its common use in laboratory experiments, and because it is a potential cushion gas in new storage sites \citep{Zamehrian2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Using X-ray computed tomography (XCT), we captured pore-scale fluid distribution within fractures during steady-state drainage.
elements.sent-2411-046: The recorded pressure fluctuations are analyzed to identify frequency of flow intermittency.
elements.sent-2411-047: By comparing the relative permeability and flow behavior of hydrogen, methane, and nitrogen, this study contributes to a deeper understanding of gas-brine interactions in fractured systems and offers critical data to improve the accuracy of field-scale UHS models.
elements.sent-2411-048: A partially karstified limestone from the Viséan (Dinantian) age was drilled from well DZH24 at the underground natural gas storage facility in Loenhout, Belgium.
elements.sent-2411-049: This limestone belongs to the Carboniferous group in the Campine-Brabant Basin of northern Belgium.
elements.sent-2411-050: The rock matrix exhibits very low porosity ($< $0.01) and permeability ($< $1 mD), indicating that fluid flow predominantly occurs through its fracture network.
elements.sent-2411-051: Previous reservoir characterization has shown that joints and partially open veins contribute to the overall permeability \cite{VanderVoet2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Since naturally fractured samples were unavailable for this study, artificially fractured samples were used as the best alternative for conducting our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-053: A cylindrical rock core, measuring \SI{25 {\milli\metre in diameter and \SI{45 {\milli\metre in length, was drilled for the experiments in this study.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The core was wrapped in Teflon tape and fractured using the Brazilian tensile stress method at Ghent University’s concrete lab.
elements.sent-2411-055: This technique was selected to generate a fracture network that closely mimics natural conditions, with variable fracture width and surface roughness as shown in Figure \ref{DryScan_3DRender .}
elements.sent-2411-056: The dry fracture image and the porosity distribution along the core are shown in Figure \ref{DryScan_Porosity , with the average measured porosity being 0.011.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Flow through rough fractures is governed by variable aperture and relative roughness, characterized by the roughness index, $\lambda_b = \frac{<b>^\sigma {<b>}$, where $<b>^\sigma$ represents the standard deviation of the aperture distribution, and $<b>$ is the mean aperture.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Aperture was obtained from the segmented dry scan using the `PoreSpy' Python toolkit \cite{Gostick2019 , which calculates local thickness as shown in Figure \ref{Thickness_885 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The aperture distribution is shown in Figure \ref{Aperture , with a mean aperture $<b>$ of \SI{350 {\micro\metre .}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The relative roughness index was calculated to be 0.61.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Dry_3DRender.png} \caption{(left) A center slice of dry scan image of the rock core. (right) 3D rendering of fracture network of the rock core.} \label{DryScan_3DRender}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Raw_Thickness.png} \caption{} \label{Thickness_885} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_LocalThickness.png} \caption{} \label{Aperture} \end{subfigure} \caption{(a) A 2D local thickness map of a horizontal slice of dry scan. (b) Aperture (local thickness) distribution with $<b>$ = \SI{350.}{\micro\metre}.} \label{Aperture_distribution}
elements.sent-2411-061: The wetting phase was prepared by dissolving 25 wt$\%$ potassium iodide (Sigma Aldrich) in deionized water, which enhanced X-ray contrast for imaging purposes.
elements.sent-2411-062: The brine was selected to provide optimal contrast for X-ray imaging.
elements.sent-2411-063: While its composition differs from the natural reservoir brine, key properties influencing relative permeability, such as surface tension and viscosity, remain comparable and should not significantly impact the results.
elements.sent-2411-064: As non-wetting phases, high-purity ($>$99.99$\%$) H$_2$, CH$_4$ and N$_2$ gases (supplied by Air Liquide) were used.
elements.sent-2411-065: The thermophysical properties of these gases are listed in Table \ref{thermophysical+properties and parameter space used in this study are shown in Figure \ref{Ca_M .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: We maintained the same flow rate instead of matching the capillary number, as the reservoir properties are fixed.
elements.sent-2411-067: Given that nitrogen and methane are used as cushion gases, it is more practical to keep the injection rate consistent rather than focusing on the capillary number.
elements.sent-2411-068: Notably, the capillary number for all flow rates and gases is less than 10$^{-5}$, indicating that the flow is capillary-dominated and exhibits similar flow regimes.
elements.sent-2411-069: It should be noted that the experimental temperature does not match the reservoir temperature due to technical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-070: However, both hydrogen and methane remain in the gaseous state, and nitrogen remains in the supercritical state under the conditions used in this study, as they would at full reservoir conditions.
elements.sent-2411-071: Consequently, the findings of this study can be reasonably extended to actual reservoir conditions.
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{Thermophysical properties of fluids at temperature 295K and pressure 100 bar} \begin{tabular}{c c c c c } Fluid Density Viscosity Viscosity ratio Interfacial tension kg m$^{-3}$ Pa s $M$ N m$^{-1}$ H$_2$ \cite{Hosseini2022} 7 9.8 x 10$^{-6}$ 0.01 0.072 CH$_4$ \cite{Bjorkvik2023} 61 1.44 x 10$^{-5}$ 0.016 0.05 N$_2$ \cite{Chow2016} 112 1.5 x 10$^{-4}$ 0.16 0.067 \label{thermophysical+properties} \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{setup.png} \caption{Experimental set-up for two-phase hydrogen/methane/nitrogen-brine core floods performed with X-ray micro computed tomography scanner.} \label{setup}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_Parameters.png} \caption{} \label{Ca_M} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_Porosity.png} \caption{} \label{DryScan_Porosity} \end{subfigure} \caption{(a) Capillary number (Ca$_t$) and viscosity ratio ($M$) used in this study. (b) Porosity along the length of the core $<\Phi> = 0.011$.} \label{Parameter_Porosity}
elements.sent-2411-072: The experimental setup is shown in figure \ref{setup .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Brine and gas are co-injected at increasing gas fractional flows to measure the drainage relative permeability, while keeping the constant total flow rate of 0.1 ml min$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Brine (wetting phase) is delivered using dual syringe Brine pump (Vindum Engineering VP12K).
elements.sent-2411-075: Gas (nonwetting phase) is injected using a syringe Gas pump (ISCO 500D pump).
elements.sent-2411-076: Due to highly flammable nature of hydrogen and methane, the gas pump was stored inside a low-oxygen ($<$ 4 $\%$) safety cabinet.
elements.sent-2411-077: The detailed experimental procedure is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-078: The core was then wrapped in aluminum foil to prevent leakage of hydrogen gas through rubber sleeve.
elements.sent-2411-079: Absolute permeability was then determined using Darcy's law as described in equation \ref{permeability .}
elements.sent-2411-080: The pressure gradient was monitored at steady-state using a Keller-33PDX pressure transducer.
elements.sent-2411-081: Given the extended duration of the experiments—spanning several days—and the potential for slight alterations in fracture morphology under high-pressure flow conditions, we measured the absolute permeability prior to each set of experiments with different gases.
elements.sent-2411-082: The measured permeability values were 8.5 D for hydrogen, 7.7 D for methane, and 3.5 D for nitrogen. \begin{equation}q = {-\frac {k A}{ \mu}} \frac {dP}{L} \label{permeability}\end{equation} where, $q$ is volumetric flow rate, $k$ is absolute permeability, $A$ is cross-sectional area of fracture, $\mu$ is viscosity of the fluid injected, dP is differential pressure across the sample and $L$ is the length of sample.
elements.sent-2411-083: For each fractional flow, sample is scanned after steady-state is reached.
elements.sent-2411-084: The steady-state is confirmed by constant differential pressure across the length of rock sample.
elements.sent-2411-085: Fractional flow of gas is defined by equation \ref{fractionaflow .}
elements.sent-2411-086: The capillary number, $Ca_t$ for each fractional flow is defined as equation \ref{Ca_definition \cite{Spurin2019a and is given in table \ref{Ca for each experiment in this study. \begin{equation}f_g = {\frac {q_g}{q_g + q_w} } \label{fractionaflow}\end{equation} where, f$_g$ is gas fractional flow, q$_g$ is gas volumetric flow rate and q$_w$ is brine volumetric flow rate. \begin{equation}Ca_t = {\frac {q_t}{\sigma (\frac{f_g}{\mu_{nw}} + \frac{1-f_g}{\mu_w})}} \label{Ca_definition}\end{equation} where, Ca$_t$ is total capillary number at fractional flow during co-injection, q$_t$ is total volumetric flow rate (q$_g$ + q$_w$), $\mu_{nw}$ is viscosity of non-wetting phase and $\mu_w$ is viscosity of wetting phase.}}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Capillary number for each fractional flow for different gas-brine systems.} \begin{tabular}{ c c c c c p{2cm} } f$_g$ q$_g$(ml min$^{-1}$) q$_w$(ml min$^{-1}$) Ca$_t$ x 10$^{-7}$ H$_2$ - brine CH$_4$ - brine N$_2$ - brine 0.1 0.01 0.09 2.36 4.8 17.2 0.3 0.03 0.07 0.84 1.76 10.28 0.6 0.06 0.04 0.43 0.91 6.41 0.8 0.08 0.02 0.32 0.68 5.12 1.0 0.1 0 0.26 0.55 4.27 \label{Ca} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-087: X-ray imaging was performed with the `High Energy micro-CT Optimized for Research' scanner (HECTOR) \cite{Masschaele2013 at the center for X-ray tomography at Ghent University (UGCT).}
elements.sent-2411-088: The X-ray energy and power were 160 kV and 15 W, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-089: We acquired 1301 projections with 600 ms exposure time for each projection.
elements.sent-2411-090: For each fractional flow at steady state entire sample length is scanned with imaging field of view (FOV) 2000 x 2400 x 1400 voxels.
elements.sent-2411-091: The voxel size was \SI{30.37 {\micro\metre for each image.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The Octopus software (Tescan-XRE, Belgium) was used to reconstruct the 3D image from acquired projections.
elements.sent-2411-093: The reconstructed images were processed using Avizo (ThermoFisher scientific) software.
elements.sent-2411-094: All the images from wet/fractional flow scans were registered to the dry scan using normalized mutual information and denoised using non-local means filter with similarity value of 0.4.
elements.sent-2411-095: The fracture from the dry scan was segmented using global thresholding, which was then applied as a mask for segmenting the wet or fractional flow scans.
elements.sent-2411-096: Two fluid phases, brine and gas, were segmented from the masked wet images using histogram-based thresholding.
elements.sent-2411-097: The total porosity of the fracture was determined from the volume fraction of the segmented dry scan, while the saturation of each fluid phase was calculated along the length of the rock sample from the segmented wet scans.
elements.sent-2411-098: The relative permeability curves were derived directly from stabilized differential pressure and saturation data, as shown in Figure \ref{RelPerm .}
elements.sent-2411-099: During drainage, as the fractional flow of gas ($f_g$) increases and the capillary number decreases, the system moves to lower water saturations, resulting in better-connected gas-phase displacement.
elements.sent-2411-100: This leads to an increase in gas relative permeability ($K_{rg}$) and a decrease in brine relative permeability ($K_{rw}$).
elements.sent-2411-101: Previous studies report that hydrogen-water systems are water-wet with respect to calcite \cite{Aghaei2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-102: In such water-wet systems, gas preferentially invades regions with lower capillary entry pressures during drainage, corresponding to fracture spaces with larger apertures \cite{Karpyn2007, Phillips2023, Chen2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Figure \ref{Fluid_Occupancy_F3 illustrates gas-brine occupancy at $f_g = 0.6$ for hydrogen, methane, and nitrogen, where gas is observed occupying largest aperture spaces, while brine prefers to remain in narrower regions.}
elements.sent-2411-104: This confirms that this system is water-wet.
elements.sent-2411-105: It it consistent with previous observations in porous media, where gas invades larger pores and brine remains in narrower throats \cite{Wang2023a .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Similar trends were observed at other fractional flows, as shown in the Supplementary Information S1.
elements.figure-2411-007: [H] \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_H2run2_F3_Occupancy_um.png} \caption{} \label{Occupancy_H2repeat} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_CH4_F3_Occupancy_um.png} \caption{} \label{Occupancy_CH4} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_N2_F3_Occupancy_um.png} \caption{} \label{Occupancy_N2} \end{subfigure} \caption{Fluid occupancy at $f_g = 0.6$ for different gases: (a) H$_2$ (b) CH$_4$ (c) N$_2$. The distance is derived from the distance map of the segmented dry fracture image, calculated as twice the distance between the center and the nearest wall of the fracture.} \label{Fluid_Occupancy_F3}
elements.sent-2411-107: The relative permeability-saturation curves for the hydrogen-brine and methane-brine systems show closely aligned trends and similar endpoint saturations.
elements.sent-2411-108: Both gases exhibit low gas relative permeability ($K_{rg}$), indicating substantial phase interference caused by the heterogeneous fracture geometry (roughness) and the low viscosity ratios of the gas-brine systems (0.01 for hydrogen-brine and 0.016 for methane-brine).
elements.sent-2411-109: This behaviour suggests the occurrence of intermittent flow and snap-off events, further discussed in Section \ref{ssec:pressure_analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-110: The wetting-phase permeability ($K_{rw}$) consistently remains much higher than the gas permeability, indicating that brine stays well-connected across all fractional flows.
elements.sent-2411-111: This strong water-wet condition ensures brine continuity throughout the fracture network, even as gas saturation increases.
elements.sent-2411-112: At higher water saturation (lower fractional flows, $f_g$), the gas relative permeability ($K_{rg}$) is slightly higher for the methane-brine system compared to the hydrogen-brine system.
elements.sent-2411-113: This is attributed to the higher capillary number ($Ca_t$) at high wetting phase saturation, where viscous forces contribute more to the flow dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-114: The slightly higher viscosity ratio of the methane-brine system (0.016 versus 0.01 for hydrogen-brine) facilitates better connectivity in the methane front, resulting in higher gas permeability \cite{Spurin2019a .}
elements.sent-2411-115: However, as the water saturation decreases (e.g., $f_g = 0.8$ and $f_g = 1$), capillary forces dominate, leading the $K_{rg}$ values for hydrogen and methane to converge at similar endpoint saturations $S_w = 0.6$ for hydrogen and $S_w = 0.59$ for methane.
elements.sent-2411-116: These slight differences in relative permeability and saturation at endpoints may fall within the experimental margin of error.
elements.sent-2411-117: Notably, the saturation change from $f_g = 0.1$ to $f_g = 1$ is identical for both gases, decreasing from 0.91 to 0.6.
elements.sent-2411-118: It should be noted that the entire hydrogen-brine relative permeability experiment was repeated to assess the uncertainty in the measurements and will be discussed in section \ref{ssec:sensitivity_analysis .}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Rel_Perm_Errorbars.png} \caption{Drainage steady-state relative permeability comparison between hydrogen-brine, methane-brine and nitrogen-brine system at 10 MPa and 22$^{\circ}$C.} \label{RelPerm}
elements.sent-2411-119: The nitrogen-brine relative permeability curves show a significant difference from those of hydrogen-brine and methane-brine, specifically with nitrogen maintaining higher gas ($K_{rg}$) and water permeability ($K_{rw}$) across all fractional flows.
elements.sent-2411-120: At the endpoint where water saturation ($S_w$) converges to 0.6 for all gases, the gas relative permeability ($K_{rg}$) reaches 0.14 for nitrogen, while it is notably lower for hydrogen and methane, at 0.007 and 0.0055, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-121: This difference arises because nitrogen's higher viscosity, an order of magnitude greater than that of hydrogen and methane, increases the capillary number under the same volumetric flow rate conditions.
elements.sent-2411-122: At 10 MPa, nitrogen exists in a supercritical state, which affects its fluid behavior in the fracture system.
elements.sent-2411-123: At the initial fractional flow of $f_g = 0.1$, the wetting-phase saturation for nitrogen is notably lower than that of hydrogen or methane.
elements.sent-2411-124: To further explore the cause of the similarities and differences between the different relative permeability measurements, we investigated the average saturation and fluid distribution in the fracture space.
elements.sent-2411-125: The slice-averaged 1-D saturation profiles for hydrogen, methane, and nitrogen experiments at different fractional flows are shown in Figures \ref{Sat_H2 , \ref{Sat_CH4 , and \ref{Sat_N2 , respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: As the fractional flow of gas (f$_g$) increases, the slice-averaged brine saturation (S$_w$) across the sample decreases for all gases, indicating that more gas is occupying the fracture space.
elements.sent-2411-127: For hydrogen and methane, saturation varies between 0.3 and 1, while for nitrogen it ranges from 0.27 to 0.99, suggesting a slightly lower average saturation for nitrogen.
elements.sent-2411-128: It results from the fracture geometry being highly heterogeneous with variable aperture distribution and roughness as shown in Figure \ref{Aperture .}
elements.sent-2411-129: The dry scan, Figure \ref{DryScan_3DRender reveals secondary fractures branching from the centre of the sample, contributing to the intricate fracture network.}
elements.sent-2411-130: This complexity affects the distribution of fluid saturation across the sample, influencing gas displacement efficiency and the overall flow dynamics.
elements.figure-2411-009: [H] \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_H2run2_Saturation.png} \caption{} \label{Sat_H2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_CH4_Saturation.png} \caption{} \label{Sat_CH4} \end{subfigure} \caption{1-D slice-averaged saturation profile of brine along the length of the core for different gases (a) H$_2$ (b) CH$_4$.} \label{Saturation_H2_CH4}
elements.figure-2411-010: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_N2_Saturation.png} \caption{1-D slice-averaged saturation profile of brine along the length of the core for different fractional flows for N$_2$.} \label{Sat_N2}
elements.sent-2411-131: At lowest fractional flows f$_g$ = 0.1, the saturation profiles illustrated in Figure \ref{Sat_fg01 shows noticeable differences, with brine saturation for hydrogen being 5$\%$ higher than for methane and 10$\%$ higher than for nitrogen.}
elements.sent-2411-132: This variation primarily results from hydrogen's lower viscosity, which leads to less efficient invasion of the fractures, ultimately resulting in lower gas saturation and higher wetting-phase saturation.
elements.sent-2411-133: Although the fluid properties of hydrogen and methane at 10 MPa and 22°C are similar, nitrogen, in its supercritical state, demonstrates distinctly different flow behavior.
elements.sent-2411-134: The higher viscosity of nitrogen facilitates more efficient invasion of the fracture network, resulting in higher gas saturation.
elements.sent-2411-135: Nitrogen is observed to drain more efficiently compared to hydrogen and methane in porous media, as also reported by \citet{Higgs2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-136: Despite the interfacial tension (IFT) of nitrogen (0.07 N m$^{-1}$) and hydrogen (0.069 N m$^{-1}$) being comparable at 10 MPa, the differences in viscosity significantly influence wetting-phase saturation at onset of drainage.
elements.sent-2411-137: This underscores the finding that at low fractional flows, the viscosity ratio plays a more pronounced role in gas displacement behavior.
elements.sent-2411-138: The fluid distribution in the fracture at f$_g$ = 0.6, as shown in Figure \ref{FluidDistribution_F3 , indicates that nitrogen has the highest gas fraction (S$_w$ = 0.76) in the fracture, followed by methane (S$_w$ = 0.8), and then hydrogen (S$_w$ = 0.76).}
elements.sent-2411-139: At higher gas fractional flows (f$_g$ = 0.8 and 1) the saturation converges for all gases as shown in Figure \ref{Sat_fg1 .}
elements.sent-2411-140: At these higher gas fractional flows, the capillary number decreases resulting in more connected gas which diminishes the impact of the viscosity ratio.
elements.sent-2411-141: Consequently, similar saturations are observed at f$_g$ = 1, representing endpoint saturation.
elements.sent-2411-142: Although the nitrogen case has a significantly higher Ca (1.2 x 10$^{-6}$) than hydrogen and methane (7.0 x 10$^{-6}$ and 1.5 x 10$^{-6}$ respectively), the end point saturations remain consistent for all gases at S$_w$ = 0.6.
elements.sent-2411-143: This suggests that the effect of capillary number on overall saturation is relatively minor under the tested conditions.
elements.figure-2411-011: [H] \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_Fg01_Saturation.png} \caption{} \label{Sat_fg01} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_Fg1_Saturation.png} \caption{} \label{Sat_fg1} \end{subfigure} \caption{1-D slice-averaged saturation profile of brine along the length of the core for different gases (a) f$_g$ = 0.1 (b) f$_g$ = 1.} \label{Saturation_different gases}
elements.figure-2411-012: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Volume_F3.png} \caption{Fluid distribution in the fracture at f$_g$=0.6 (left) H$_2$, (middle) CH$_4$, (right) N$_2$.} \label{FluidDistribution_F3}
elements.sent-2411-144: The fluid distribution for the hydrogen-brine system in the fracture exhibits signs of intermittency at higher water saturations (lower $f_g$), as illustrated in Figure \ref{FluidDistribution_intermittency for $f_g = 0.1$.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Subtle differences in gas distribution are observed between two scans taken 30 minutes apart after reaching steady-state pressure.
elements.sent-2411-146: Several locations are identified by coloured circles: Circle 1 (yellow) marks a region where gas disappears in the subsequent scan, while Circles 2 (green) indicate location where gas reappears.
elements.sent-2411-147: These fluctuations in the gas phase correspond to capillary events and are also reflected in the measured differential pressure.
elements.sent-2411-148: In contrast, the nitrogen-brine system exhibits fewer fluctuations in saturation, suggesting a more stable flow pattern.
elements.sent-2411-149: A more detailed analysis of pressure fluctuations, intermittency, and their impact on fluid distribution will be discussed in Section \ref{ssec:pressure_analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-150: Along the length of the fracture, slice-averaged saturation reaches S$_w$ = 1 for many slices, indicating a disconnected gas phase.
elements.sent-2411-151: Figure \ref{Sat_H2_intermittency_fg01 presents the 1-D slice-averaged saturation profiles for these two subsequent scans revealing significant fluctuations in saturation within this period, emphasizing dynamic capillary processes occurring in the fracture.}
elements.sent-2411-152: These processes are not fully captured in terms of intermediate gray values due to the scanner's limited temporal resolution.
elements.sent-2411-153: As the gas fractional flow increases to f$_g$ = 0.8, the gas phase becomes more connected throughout the fracture, which reduces intermittency and enhances overall gas saturation as demonstrated by the 3D volume rendering of fluid distribution in Figure \ref{FluidDistribution_F1F4 .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Intermittency significantly impacts relative permeability, resulting in notably low gas permeabilities, particularly for hydrogen and methane as discussed in Section \ref{ssec:relperm .}
elements.sent-2411-155: We note here that the observed intermittency may also be influenced by the dissolution of hydrogen and methane into the brine during the experiment, as the injected gas and brine were not pre-equilibrated, a phenomenon previously described by \cite{Boon2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-156: However, the reappearance of gas in the fracture downstream indicates that intermittency is a genuine effect and not solely attributed to dissolution.
elements.sent-2411-157: The solubilities of hydrogen, methane, and nitrogen in water are 0.8 mg L$^{-1}$, 22.7 mg L$^{-1}$, and 10 mg L$^{-1}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-158: Hydrogen’s significantly lower solubility, yet comparable phase disconnectivity, suggests that dissolution effects are likely minimal.
elements.figure-2411-013: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Intermittancy_F1_H2run2.png} \caption{Fluid distribution in fracture of hydrogen-brine flow at f$_g$ = 0.1 for two scans taken 30 minutes apart.} \label{FluidDistribution_intermittency}
elements.figure-2411-014: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_intermittency_H2run2Fg01_Saturation.png} \caption{1-D slice-averaged saturation profile of hydrogen-brine flow at f$_g$ = 0.1 for two scans taken 30 minutes apart.} \label{Sat_H2_intermittency_fg01}
elements.figure-2411-015: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Fluids_H2_run2_F1F4.png} \caption{H$_2$-brine distribution in the fracture (left) f$_g$ = 0.1 (right) f$_g$ = 0.8.} \label{FluidDistribution_F1F4}
elements.sent-2411-159: The lower gas relative permeabilities for similar global water saturation suggest higher phase interference in the fracture.
elements.sent-2411-160: Saturation and fluid distribution in the fracture, based on scans taken 30 minutes apart, indicate the presence of intermittency, where Haines jumps and snap-off events occur.
elements.sent-2411-161: Since the scan duration is 16 minutes and these events appear to happen much more rapidly, we observed pressure data recorded at a much higher time resolution of 1 second.
elements.sent-2411-162: The fluctuations in differential pressure measurements from the transducer across the rock samples provide insights into the timescale and the fluctuations that correspond to snap-off and Haines jumps occurring during two-phase displacements \cite{Spurin2022, Wang2023a .}
elements.sent-2411-163: Differential pressure measurements at $f_g = 0.6$ over a 30-minute period for H$_2$, CH$_4$, and N$_2$ are presented in Figure \ref{Pressuredrop .}
elements.sent-2411-164: These data reveal fluctuations over varied timescales for each gas, underscoring distinct dynamics associated with each gas phase.
elements.sent-2411-165: There are notable differences in the amplitude and frequency of these oscillations, particularly between H$_2$ and CH$_4$ on one hand and N$_2$ on the other.
elements.sent-2411-166: The pressure amplitude of fluctuations is greater for hydrogen and methane compared to nitrogen, with the fluctuations occurring over timescales of approximately 150 seconds for hydrogen, 30 seconds for methane, and one second for nitrogen.
elements.sent-2411-167: The pressure fluctuations within the pores result in intermittent pore filling, occurring over a broad range of timescales.
elements.sent-2411-168: At different fractional flows, the timescales of these fluctuations vary, reflecting changes in the connectivity of the gas phase.
elements.sent-2411-169: The additional pressure data and preliminary analysis, including the Gaussian distribution of fluctuations and red noise, are provided in Supplementary Information S2.
elements.figure-2411-016: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_PressH2run2_F3.png} \caption{Differential pressure data during steady-state for f$_g$ = 0.6.} \label{Pressuredrop}
elements.sent-2411-170: As discussed in previous sections, relative permeabilities at reservoir conditions in fractures are strongly influenced by intermittency, leading to significant fluctuations in pressure and saturation.
elements.sent-2411-171: To assess the sensitivity of the hydrogen-brine system under these conditions, we repeated the experiment under identical flow conditions.
elements.sent-2411-172: Overall, qualitative trends in the relative permeability-saturation curves remained consistent across both runs, as shown in Figure \ref{RelPerm_sensitivity .}
elements.sent-2411-173: The water permeabilities ($K_{rw}$) were quantitatively similar in both runs.
elements.sent-2411-174: Gas permeability, while consistently low across runs, exhibited slight differences, likely due to minor pressure fluctuation variations.
elements.sent-2411-175: Since gas permeabilities are very low, even slight variations in pressure fluctuations can result in differences in the measured relative gas permeability.
elements.sent-2411-176: The pressure fluctuations for these two runs at $f_g = 0.6$ are shown in Figure \ref{RelPerm_press_sensitivity_fg06 .}
elements.sent-2411-177: The data clearly exhibits cyclic fluctuations, with the timescales of these fluctuations differing between the two runs: in the first run, the period was 0.5 minutes, while in the repeated run, the period extended to 2.5 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-178: These variations in fluctuation timescales may contribute to slight differences in the calculated relative gas permeabilities.
elements.sent-2411-179: Such differences could arise due to slight changes in fracture morphology, potentially occurring during pressurization, as the fracture is sensitive to applied stress.
elements.sent-2411-180: Over the course of running different experiments presented in this paper , the rock sample underwent pressurization-depressurization cycles, potentially impacting fracture morphology.
elements.sent-2411-181: This is reflected in the observed decrease in absolute permeability before the experimental runs, from 23 D to 8 D.
elements.sent-2411-182: Despite these slight variations, the overall behavior remains consistent across both experimental runs, confirming that our workflow is robust and validating the results presented in this study.
elements.sent-2411-183: The differential pressure data for other fractional flows are provided in Supplementary Information S3.
elements.figure-2411-017: [H] \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_sensitivity.png} \caption{} \label{Relperm_sensitivity} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{RelPerm_Press_Fg06_sensitivity.png} \caption{} \label{RelPerm_press_sensitivity_fg06} \end{subfigure} \caption{ (a) Relative permeability of hydrogen-brine system for two runs. (b) Differential pressure recorded over 30 minutes for two runs of hydrogen-brine experiment.} \label{RelPerm_sensitivity}
elements.sent-2411-184: Direct measurements of relative permeability for hydrogen compared to other gases in fractured rock samples are currently absent from the literature.
elements.sent-2411-185: Here, we compare our findings with existing experimental data and numerical models for different fluid systems in rough fractures, as shown in Figure \ref{RelPerm_Fracture .}
elements.sent-2411-186: Compared to previous studies, the hydrogen gas relative permeability in our system is notably lower, indicating interference effects that are stronger than predicted by existing models such as \cite{Watanabe2015 , \citet{Gong2021 , and \citet{Bertels2001 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: The limited available experimental data were collected under different flow conditions and using different fluids, making direct comparisons challenging.
elements.sent-2411-188: The fluid pairs used in various studies from the literature are summarized in Supplementary Information S4.
elements.sent-2411-189: The nitrogen permeability aligns more closely with the model proposed by \citet{Gong2021 ; however, the impact of fracture geometry could not be fully resolved based on these comparisons.}
elements.sent-2411-190: The nitrogen-brine system relative permeabilities were also compared to models from the literature, with detailed discussions provided in the Supplementary Information S4.
elements.sent-2411-191: In contrast, water permeability in our results aligns more closely with the model suggested by \citet{Piri2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-192: Importantly, fracture morphology also influences these differences, so while such comparisons are informative, they are not definitive.
elements.sent-2411-193: Controlled experiments and models that account for specific fracture morphology in hydrogen-brine systems are essential for more conclusive insights.
elements.figure-2411-018: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Rel_Perm_comparisonH2_Fracture.png} \caption{Comparison of relative permeability measurements with previous studies in fracture.} \label{RelPerm_Fracture}
elements.sent-2411-194: The lower hydrogen gas permeability observed in our fracture study is consistent with trends seen in porous media; thus, we also compared our measurements with direct hydrogen permeability data from porous media studies in Berea sandstone \cite{Lysyy2022, Boon2022 and Bentheimer sandstone \cite{Higgs2023 , shown in Figure \ref{RelPerm_Porous .}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Despite marked differences in sample geometry and flow conditions, the results remain qualitatively comparable, as all experiments were conducted at low capillary numbers (Ca$_t$).
elements.sent-2411-196: In porous media, the relative permeability curves from \citet{Higgs2023 exhibit closer alignment for hydrogen, methane, and nitrogen.}
elements.sent-2411-197: However, our fracture study reveals greater differences in both wetting- and non-wetting-phase permeabilities.
elements.sent-2411-198: This divergence can likely be attributed to the increased intermittency in the fracture, resulting from the higher pressure conditions (100 bar) in our study, where the interfacial tension (IFT) between hydrogen and methane differs more significantly, and nitrogen exists in a supercritical state.
elements.sent-2411-199: In contrast, the experiments by \citet{Higgs2023 , conducted at 20 bar, were performed under conditions where the fluid properties of these gases were more similar.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Additionally, our study shows a much higher irreducible saturation, exemplifying the dependence of capillary-dominated relative permeability curves on flow rate (injection rate) and geometry (porous vs. fractured).
elements.sent-2411-201: When comparing our hydrogen permeability results to those from \citet{Boon2022 , who also conducted high-pressure experiments (100 bar) at 18$^{\circ}$C under similar conditions, hydrogen and water permeabilities were generally higher across most saturations for porous media, though the general trend remains consistent.}
elements.sent-2411-202: These comparisons highlight the increased complexity of fluid interactions in fractures compared to those observed in porous media.
elements.figure-2411-019: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Rel_Perm_comparison_PorousMedia.png} \caption{Comparison of relative permeability measurements with previous hydrogen measurement studies in porous media. The black lines denote the hydrogen-brine data from our study.} \label{RelPerm_Porous}
elements.sent-2411-203: The findings from our hydrogen-brine and methane-brine experiments, along with nitrogen-brine experiment, provide crucial insights for hydrogen storage in subsurface fractured reservoirs and particularly in the case of transitioning existing natural gas storage facilities.
elements.sent-2411-204: Our experiments are focused on the Loenhout storage site in northern Belgium which is a part of the BeHyStore project.
elements.sent-2411-205: We conducted direct measurements of hydrogen, methane, and nitrogen relative permeabilities in fractured rock under reservoir pressure conditions (10 MPa), while recognizing that experimental limitations prevented matching the reservoir temperature.
elements.sent-2411-206: However, the impact of temperature on relative permeability is expected to be minimal.
elements.sent-2411-207: Our study summarizes three key findings relevant to hydrogen storage in fractured reservoirs:.
elements.sent-2411-208: The minor differences observed are attributed to methane’s higher viscosity and lower interfacial tension (0.05 N m$^{-1}$ for methane versus 0.07 N m$^{-1}$ for hydrogen).
elements.sent-2411-209: This behavior is not well represented by existing models, highlighting the need for more advanced modeling approaches to accurately capture the dynamics of hydrogen and methane in fractured media.
elements.sent-2411-210: It also influences considerations for its use as a cushion gas due to its propensity for breakthrough issues during injection and production.
elements.sent-2411-211: In underground gas storage, tight shale caprock typically acts as a barrier, but fractures, whether preexisting or induced by pressure variations during hydrogen injection, can provide additional pathways for gas movement.
elements.sent-2411-212: The low gas relative permeability observed in this study may contribute to the mitigation of leakage risks supporting the long-term stability and security of hydrogen storage.
elements.sent-2411-213: In conclusion, this study provides critical insights into hydrogen behavior in fractured rock, supporting efforts to optimize underground hydrogen storage.
elements.sent-2411-214: By highlighting the limitations of using nitrogen as a proxy for hydrogen, our findings emphasize the need for hydrogen-specific research to develop effective storage strategies and safety protocols in subsurface reservoirs.
elements.sent-2411-215: Future work should focus on measuring hydrogen-brine relative permeability in fracture samples with varied aperture sizes and roughness.
elements.sent-2411-216: Such studies will be valuable in understanding the impact of fracture geometry on flow behavior, ultimately enhancing the accuracy of upscaled fracture network models for reservoir-scale applications.
elements.sent-2411-217: This study was financed in part by Fluxys, Belgium.
elements.sent-2411-218: We also acknowledge research funding from the Belgian Federal Energy Transition Fund (project BE-HyStore), the Research Foundation-Flanders (FWO, project G004820N and G51418N) and Ghent University's Special Research Fund (BOF/COR/2022/008).

elements.sent-2411-229: The steady-state pressures for different fractional flows were measured using a differential pressure transducer (PD-33X) and are presented in Figures \ref{Diff_Press_H2 , \ref{Diff_Press_CH4 , and \ref{Diff_Press_N2 for two runs each of the hydrogen, methane, and nitrogen experiments, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-230: Steady-state was achieved within 3 hours for the initial fractional flow, while subsequent fractional flows reached steady-state within 2 hours.
elements.sent-2411-231: This equilibration time is significantly shorter compared to traditional core-flood experiments in porous media.

elements.sent-2411-232: Figure \ref{Saturation_sensitivity shows saturation profiles from two experimental runs at two different gas fractional flows.}
elements.sent-2411-233: At a lower fractional flow (f$_g$ = 0.1), the saturation profiles exhibited significant variation, indicating greater intermittency in fluid distribution.
elements.sent-2411-234: With an increase in fractional flow to $f_g$ = 1, the saturation profiles became more consistent, as illustrated in Figure \ref{Sat_sensitivity_fg1 .}
elements.sent-2411-235: The average saturation variation between the two runs was 6\% for $f_g$ = 0.1 and 3\% for $f_g$ = 1.
elements.sent-2411-236: These observations suggest that the two-phase hydrogen-brine flow system is more susceptible to fluctuations at lower capillary numbers, where capillary forces strongly influence the flow.

elements.section-2411-010: S4: Nitrogen relative permeability : comparison to literature
elements.sent-2411-237: The nitrogen-brine relative permeability is compared with experimental and numerical data from the literature for fractured media.
elements.sent-2411-238: The gas permeability for the nitrogen-brine system closely aligns with predictions by \citet{Gong2021 for water-oil system, whereas the water permeability is lower than the model's prediction at the same water saturation.}
elements.figure-2411-026: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Rel_Perm_comparisonN2_Fracture.png} \caption{Comparison of nitrogen relative permeability from this study (black lines) with relative permeability models and experimental data for fractured media available in the literature.} \label{RelPerm_N2_Fracture}
elements.sent-2411-239: Table \ref{Fluid_systems lists the different fluid pairs used in this study and those from the literature for comparison.}
